title: The nonpareil combing pattern
subtitle: The foundational two-pass comb stroke for European marbled paper
excerpt: The nonpareil is the entry-level pattern for European marbling: a base of floated colours is drawn into parallel stripes by a wide-tooth comb, then a second finer-tooth comb pass at right angles creates the small-scale ripple that gives the pattern its name. It is the starting point for almost all more complex marbling patterns.
sourceNotes: C. W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1853, 1881), documents the nonpareil as a foundational pattern of European marbling. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/artofmarbling00wool. The term 'nonpareil' appears consistently in nineteenth-century marbling trade literature to denote this specific two-pass comb pattern.

glossaryTerms.definition: A two-pass comb pattern in European paper marbling. The first comb pass draws parallel stripes through the floated colours; the second, perpendicular pass creates the small-scale ripple that is the nonpareil's defining feature.
One movement of a marbling comb across the full length or width of the size surface. Each pass redistributes the floating colours to build the pattern.

content.text: is the pattern beginners learn first in European marbling, because it is reproducible and sets up more complex patterns such as the peacock and the bouquet. It requires two

: the first creates stripes, the second creates ripples within those stripes. The carrageenan bath must be prepared and colours tested before beginning; see the carrageenan-bath marbling tutorial for that setup.

Drop colours onto the settled size in parallel rows or random scatter, starting with the lightest colours and working toward the darkest. Each later drop pushes earlier drops outward. The base pattern before combing should cover the size surface with roughly equal amounts of each colour. Gaps in the coverage will show as uncoloured areas in the final print. Use three to five colours for a classic nonpareil; more produces a busier result.

Place the first comb at one short end of the tray with the teeth just touching the size surface. Draw it steadily across the full length of the tray in one smooth stroke, maintaining a constant speed. The colours elongate into parallel stripes behind the comb teeth. Any hesitation or speed change causes irregular stripe widths. Do not stop mid-pull. A walking pace is about right.

Place the second comb at one long edge of the tray, perpendicular to the first set of stripes. Use a finer-tooth comb than the first: teeth spaced 5 to 8 mm apart, compared to the first comb's 20 mm spacing. Draw the second comb steadily across the short dimension of the tray. The perpendicular stroke ripples the stripes into the nonpareil wave. Lift the print immediately after this second pass.

attrs.title: Comb tooth spacing
attrs.body: A first comb with 20 mm tooth spacing and a second comb with 5 to 8 mm spacing produces the classic nonpareil scale. Wider first-comb spacing creates bold waves; narrower second-comb spacing creates a tighter ripple.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems specific to the nonpareil combing pattern.

items.symptom: The stripes break up into dots rather than continuous lines
items.cause: The comb lifted unevenly from the size surface during the pull, or moved too quickly.
items.fix: Hold the comb absolutely level so all teeth contact the size simultaneously. Move at a steady walking pace. A comb with teeth of uneven length will always produce broken stripes; check the comb before use.

items.symptom: The second-pass ripple is barely visible in the finished print
items.cause: The second comb tooth spacing is too wide relative to the stripe width from the first pass.
items.fix: Use a noticeably finer comb for the second pass. The ripple only reads clearly when the second-comb teeth are significantly closer together than the stripe width produced by the first pass.
